claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

How old is the child you're claiming for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I actually have three children I'd like to check on - Alex is 5, Blake is 21, and Charlie is 13.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! It's great to have all the information upfront. Let me assess eligibility for each of your three children: Alex (5), Blake (21), and Charlie (13).

Let me start by checking the eligibility path for each child based on their ages:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I now have a clearer picture. Here's what I can tell you so far:

---

**Blake (21 years old):** Unfortunately, Blake is **not eligible** for Child Benefit. Children must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. At 21, Blake no longer qualifies.

---

**Alex (5) and Charlie (13):** Since both are under 16, they potentially qualify, but I need to check a few more things.

**For Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 13), I have a question:**

Are both Alex and Charlie working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

*(This is typically "yes" for young children, but I need to confirm for the assessment.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - neither Alex nor Charlie work at all. They're just kids at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, they're not working. Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit themselves. *(This would be very unusual for children their ages, but I need to confirm.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits themselves. They're just regular kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie in an apprenticeship?**

Since they're at school, I assume the answer is no, but just to confirm - neither of them is enrolled in any kind of apprenticeship programme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not in any apprenticeships - they're just at primary and secondary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, living in Manchester qualifies you for the residency requirement. Now let's check your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents) at least equal to the Child Benefit amount
3. **You pay maintenance** for them under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Charlie live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Please let me know if:
- **No one else is claiming** for them
- **Someone else was claiming but has agreed to transfer the claim to you**
- **There's a dispute** about who should receive the benefit

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Now I need to check if any special circumstances apply to where Alex and Charlie are living.

**Are Alex and Charlie in any of the following situations?**

1. **In hospital** for an extended period
2. **In local authority care**
3. **Being fostered**
4. **Living normally at home** (none of the above)

I assume they're living normally at home with you, but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Charlie has been in care for a couple of months, but they come home on the weekends to stay with me. Alex is just living at home normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex and Charlie are in different situations, I'll need to assess them separately.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is helpful. Let me ask about both children:

**For Alex (living at home normally):**
Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

**For Charlie (in care):**
You mentioned Charlie has been in care for a couple of months but comes home on weekends. I need to check a couple of things:

1. **Does Charlie spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?** (Weekend visits could meet this - for example, staying Friday evening to Sunday would typically be more than 24 hours)

2. **Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - no, the council isn't paying anything for them.

For Charlie - yes, they come home on the weekends and spend more than 24 hours with me. And yes, the local authority gives me maintenance for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details. This gives me what I need to determine eligibility for both children.

**For Alex:** Great news - since the council isn't paying anything for Alex, they should qualify.

**For Charlie:** Although Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you (which is good), the fact that the local authority is paying maintenance for Charlie affects eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information needed to give you a final eligibility outcome for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Child Benefit Eligibility Summary

Here's the outcome of the assessment for your three children:

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 5 | ✅ **YES** | Meets all eligibility requirements |
| **Blake** | 21 | ❌ **NO** | Over the maximum age limit (must be under 20) |
| **Charlie** | 13 | ❌ **NO** | Local authority is paying maintenance |

---

### Next Steps for Alex

Since Alex is eligible, you can apply for Child Benefit by:

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate)
4. Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

⚠️ **Important:** If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, you may be affected by the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid through tax.

---

### About Charlie

If Charlie's situation changes in the future—for example, if they return home permanently and the local authority stops paying maintenance—you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for them at that point. It's worth keeping this in mind.

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the requirements: they are under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you (the claimant) live in the UK, you live with Alex and are responsible for them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. To qualify, a child must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. At 21 years old, Blake exceeds the maximum age limit and therefore does not qualify as a 'child' for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit at this time. Although Charlie is under 16 and meets most eligibility criteria, they are currently in local authority care and the council is paying maintenance for them. When a local council is paying towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for that child, even if the child spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of your three children, only Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is too old at 21 to qualify, and Charlie cannot be claimed for while the local authority is paying maintenance. If Charlie's care situation changes in the future and the council stops paying maintenance, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them at that point.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the requirements: they are under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you (the claimant) live in the UK, you live with Alex and are responsible for them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. To qualify, a child must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. At 21 years old, Blake exceeds the maximum age limit and therefore does not qualify as a 'child' for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit at this time. Although Charlie is under 16 and meets most eligibility criteria, they are currently in local authority care and the council is paying maintenance for them. When a local council is paying towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for that child, even if the child spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you.

response.overall_reasoning: Of your three children, only Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is too old at 21 to qualify, and Charlie cannot be claimed for while the local authority is paying maintenance. If Charlie's care situation changes in the future and the council stops paying maintenance, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them at that point.
